name: Omar ibn al-Khattab
title: Omar ibn al-Khattab (RA): The Convert Who Changed Everything
meta_description: The story of Omar ibn al-Khattab (RA), who set out one night to kill the Prophet Muhammad and ended the night as a believer. How his conversion turned the tide for the early Muslims.
keywords: Omar ibn al-Khattab, story of Omar, Al-Farooq, conversion of Omar, second caliph, companions of the Prophet, sahaba stories
lede: He left his house one night with a drawn sword and one intention: to find the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and end his message for good. He did not hide it. He said it out loud as he walked.

This is the story of Omar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him): a man feared in Makkah for his strength, who set out to kill the Prophet ﷺ and ended the same night kneeling in front of him as a believer.

sections.h2: A man you would step aside for
paras: Omar was from Banu Adi, a small clan of Quraysh. He was tall and powerfully built, so strong that his feet were said to brush the ground when he rode. He had deep-set eyes, large hands, and a voice so deep that people describe a barber once fainting from fright when Omar spoke to him.

When he received revelation, the Prophet ﷺ was a young man, and Omar was younger still, in his early twenties. He had grown up herding the sheep and camels of his father and his aunts in the valleys around Makkah, going home to a handful of dates that had to last the day.

His father was known for one thing above all: harshness. He overworked Omar and beat him, and was cruel to everyone around him. Omar could have inherited that cruelty and called it his birthright. Instead he carried out of it a deep care for justice, and he never forgot that he had once been a barefoot shepherd.

Omar was not the brute people sometimes imagine. He learned to read and write as a teenager, which was rare among Quraysh, and he knew their poetry well. He won wrestling matches for money, and he won contests of words, defeating his opponents with verse.

Because he was both clever and intimidating, Quraysh made him their spokesman. When outsiders came to Makkah, Omar would stand and defend not only his own clan but all of Quraysh: their honour, their forefathers, their idols, the whole of their way of life.

So when a new message began to divide the families of Makkah, Omar took it personally. His objection was not the pure ego of his uncle Abu Jahl, who feared for his clan's standing. Omar feared for his people as a whole. To him, this message was tearing apart the only society he knew, and that is why he turned against it.

It is worth pausing on this strength, because Islam never tried to erase it. Our mother Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her) once warned against confusing piety with weakness, and pointed to Omar as the answer: he prayed more than anyone and was as humble as anyone, yet when he walked, he walked with purpose, and when he spoke, he made sure he was heard. The Prophet ﷺ would not tame Omar. He would aim him.

sections.h2: Islam kept arriving at his door
paras: Again and again, Allah placed this religion in front of Omar before he was ready to see it. One day he rode past a Muslim woman, Umm Abdillah, as she packed to flee Makkah for Abyssinia. He expected her anger. He did not expect his own response. He lowered his head and said, "May Allah be with you," and rode off with a softness in him she had never seen before.

Closer to home, his own sister Fatima had already become Muslim, and so had his brother Zayd, a quiet man who slipped into Islam without anyone noticing. Years later Zayd would be killed at the Battle of Yamama, and Omar, finding his body, would say, "You beat me twice. You beat me to Islam, and you beat me to martyrdom."

Then there was the night at the Kaaba. Omar could not find his drinking companions, so he went to make tawaf instead, and found the Prophet ﷺ praying alone in the dark. He crept under the cloth of the Kaaba to listen, and heard the Quran for the first time. He thought, this is a poet. The verse answered him: it is not the words of a poet. He thought, a soothsayer. The verse answered again. He fled, shaken, unable to make sense of what he had heard.

sections.h2: The night he set out to kill
paras: Abu Jahl stood before the leaders of Quraysh and offered a fortune to whoever would kill the Prophet ﷺ. Omar said he would do it. He drew his sword and marched through the streets, not hiding his purpose, saying as he walked that he would make Makkah what it used to be.

A man of his own clan, secretly a Muslim, met him on the way and tried to turn him aside. First he reasoned with him: was Muhammad ﷺ not known as the truthful and trustworthy one, a man who only ever did good for his people? When that did not land, he told Omar the truth that would stop him cold: "Your own sister and her husband have followed Muhammad. Set your own house in order first." Omar turned and stormed toward Fatima's home.

Behind the door, his sister and her husband were being taught the Quran. Omar burst in. When his brother-in-law tried to shield his wife, Omar struck him to the ground. Fatima tried to pull her brother off, and his hand caught her face and drew blood. The sight of his sister bleeding stopped him. He was strong, but he was not a man without conscience, and he was ashamed.

sections.h2: "What is it that Muhammad has brought?"
paras: Bleeding and unafraid, Fatima told him plainly: "Do what you want. We have become Muslim." Something in Omar gave way. He saw the pages of the Quran in the corner and asked to read them. She would not let him touch them until he had washed, and he, the man with the sword, got up and washed himself, and sat back down.

He read the opening of Surah Ta Ha: that the Quran was not sent to cause him distress, but as a reminder. He said quietly, "How beautiful these words are." Then he read on, until he reached the words that seemed to be speaking directly to him.

ayah.text: I am God; there is no god but Me. So worship Me and keep up the prayer so that you remember Me.

paras: Omar said, "The One who spoke these words has the right to be worshipped alone." Then he asked the question that turns the whole story: "Is this what Quraysh is running away from?" He asked to be taken to the Prophet ﷺ at once, his sword still at his side.

At the secret house of the believers, a man saw Omar at the door and panicked. But Hamza (may Allah be pleased with him), newly Muslim himself, said calmly to open it: if Omar came in peace, well and good, and if not, he would face Omar's own sword. The Prophet ﷺ went to meet him, took him by the garment, and brought the huge man to his knees, eye to eye. "What brought you here, son of al-Khattab?"

And Omar, kneeling, said the words he had set out that night to silence: "I have come to believe in Allah and His Messenger and what he has brought from Allah." The Prophet ﷺ called out "Allahu Akbar," and the believers in the house, who had learned to keep their voices low, shouted it with him until the small room rang.

Omar would not stay hidden. "Are we not upon the truth?" he asked. "Then why are we hiding?" That day the Prophet ﷺ named him al-Farooq, the one who separates truth from falsehood. The believers walked out of the house in two rows, one behind Hamza and one behind Omar, and prayed openly at the Kaaba for the first time.

Those who had been the most defenceless never forgot it. Abdullah ibn Masud said the conversion of Omar was itself a victory. We could not pray near the Kaaba, he said, until Omar became Muslim, and then we prayed, and when they struck us, we struck back. A man who had terrified the weak became their shield.

Omar wanted the news out. He went and told the loudest man in Makkah, so word would travel fast, and Quraysh came for him. He stood his ground and did not back down. Then he went, of all places, to the door of his uncle Abu Jahl, the man who days earlier had offered a fortune for the Prophet's life, and told him simply that he now believed in Allah and His Messenger. The door was shut in his face. It did not move him.

When the Muslims later migrated to Madinah, most left in secret. Omar made tawaf openly, prayed two units calmly, then went to the leaders of Quraysh and told them whoever wished to leave his mother weeping should follow him beyond the valley. No one came. He took the largest group of migrants out of Makkah, and none of them feared while he was among them.

This is the genius of his story. Omar took every quality that had made him dangerous, his anger, his strength, his cruelty, and turned each one toward what was good: justice for the weak, mercy, generosity, and prayer through the night.

lessons_intro: His story is the most dramatic turn in the early seerah, and yet what it asks of us is quiet: that no one, including ourselves, is beyond changing for the better.

lessons.text: People said his father's donkey would accept Islam before he would. Never write off a person, or yourself, while there is still breath and a chance to change.

lessons.bold: Strength is meant to be aimed.
lessons.text: Islam did not soften Omar into someone else. It took the very things that made him formidable and pointed them toward justice and good.

lessons.bold: You are not bound by where you came from.
lessons.text: Omar grew up under a harsh father and chose not to pass that harshness on. Our circumstances explain us; they do not have to define us.

lessons.bold: Truth is worth more than reputation.
lessons.text: Accepting the message cost Omar everything he was known for. He chose what was true over how he was seen, and never looked back.

faq.a: A leading man of Quraysh known for his strength, intelligence, and standing as the spokesman of his people. After accepting Islam he became one of the closest companions of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and, later, the second caliph.

faq.q: Why is the conversion of Omar so significant?
faq.a: He was one of the two most powerful men in Makkah and had been among those who harmed the Muslims. When he accepted Islam, the believers were able to pray openly at the Kaaba for the first time. The companion Abdullah ibn Masud called his conversion a victory in itself.

faq.q: What does the title Al-Farooq mean?
faq.a: It means the one who separates truth from falsehood. The Prophet ﷺ gave Omar this name on the day he accepted Islam and asked why the believers should keep hiding their faith.

faq.q: What can we learn from the story of Omar?
faq.a: That no one is beyond hope, that strength is meant to be directed toward good, that we are not bound by where we came from, and that truth is worth more than reputation.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is tempting to think people are fixed: the harsh stay harsh, the lost stay lost, and the person who set out to do harm will always be that person. Omar's story refuses that idea. The same man who walked the streets with a drawn sword spent his nights, years later, weeping in prayer and guarding the rights of the weak.

Most of us are not waiting to be transformed in a single night. But we all carry something we think is too set to change: a temper, a habit, an old story about who we are. Omar's life is a quiet invitation to bring that very thing to Allah and let it be turned toward good. Choose what is true over how you are seen, and aim your strength, whatever it is, at something worth it today.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Omar ibn al-Khattab (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 20:14). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

social.caption:
Omar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) left his house one night with a drawn sword, set on ending the Prophet's ﷺ message for good.

On the way he was told his own sister had become Muslim. He went to her home in a rage, struck her husband, and drew his sister's blood. Then he stopped. He asked to read the Quran for himself, and the words reached him: "I am God; there is no god but Me." The same night, the man who set out to kill ended up kneeling before the Prophet ﷺ as a believer.

His conversion let the early Muslims pray openly for the first time. A man feared by the weak became their shield.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/omar-ibn-al-khattab-4

email.subject: He set out to kill the Prophet. He ended the night a believer.
email.alt_subject: "Is this what Quraysh is running away from?"
email.preview: The story of Omar ibn al-Khattab, the convert who changed everything.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

One night in Makkah, a man left his house with a drawn sword and a single intention: to find the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and end his message for good. His name was Omar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him), and he was one of the most feared men in the city.

He did not hide his purpose. He said it out loud as he walked. But on the way, someone stopped him with the truth he was not expecting: his own sister had already become Muslim. Omar turned and stormed toward her house instead.

What happened there is hard to read. He struck her husband to the ground, and when his sister tried to pull him away, his hand caught her face and drew blood. The sight of it stopped him. He was strong, but he was not without conscience, and he was ashamed.

Bleeding, his sister told him plainly: "Do what you want. We have become Muslim." Something gave way in him. He asked to read the pages of the Quran for himself. He read that it was not sent to cause him distress, but as a reminder, and he said quietly, "How beautiful these words are." He read on until he reached: "I am God; there is no god but Me. So worship Me and keep up the prayer."

Then he asked the question that turns the whole story: "Is this what Quraysh is running away from?"

That same night, the man who had set out to kill the Prophet ﷺ went to him, sword still at his side, and knelt before him as a believer.

His conversion changed everything. For the first time, the Muslims prayed openly at the Kaaba. A man who had terrified the weak became their shield. And here is the part that stays with me: Omar did not become someone else. He took every quality that had made him dangerous, his anger, his strength, even his hardness, and aimed each one at what was good.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss: no one is beyond hope, including the parts of ourselves we think are too set to change.

May Allah be pleased with him, and let us bring what is hardest in us to Him to be turned toward good.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: One night in Makkah, a man left his house with a drawn sword. His name was Omar ibn al-Khattab, and his intention was simple: to find the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and end his message for good. He did not hide it. He said it aloud as he walked.

Omar was feared in his city. He was tall and powerfully built, clever enough to read and write when few of Quraysh could, and bold enough to be their spokesman. He had grown up a barefoot shepherd under a harsh father, and he had decided not to pass that harshness on.

On his way to kill, a man stopped him with the truth he least expected: his own sister had become Muslim. Omar turned and stormed toward her house. He found her and her husband being taught the Quran, and in his rage he struck her husband down. When his sister tried to pull him away, his hand caught her face and drew blood.

The sight of it stopped him. He was strong, but he was not a man without conscience, and he was ashamed. Bleeding, his sister told him plainly, "Do what you want. We have become Muslim." Then Omar asked to read the pages they had been reciting. She would not let him touch them until he had washed, and the man with the sword got up, washed himself, and sat back down.

He read that the Quran was not sent to cause him distress, but as a reminder, and he said quietly, "How beautiful these words are." He read on until he reached the words that seemed to speak straight to him: "I am God; there is no god but Me. So worship Me and keep up the prayer." He looked up and asked, "Is this what Quraysh is running away from?"

That same night he went to the Prophet ﷺ, his sword still at his side. The Prophet ﷺ took him by the garment and brought the huge man to his knees, eye to eye, and asked what had brought him. And Omar, kneeling, said the words he had set out to silence: "I have come to believe in Allah and His Messenger." The room rang with Allahu Akbar.

His conversion changed everything. For the first time the believers prayed openly at the Kaaba. The man who had once terrified the weak became their shield, and when he later left for Madinah, he walked out in the open while others went in secret. Years on, the same man wept through his nights in prayer.
He took every quality that had made him dangerous and aimed it at what was good. That is why his name still stirs something in the hearts of those who hear it.
storybook.pullquote: Is this what Quraysh is running away from?
storybook.reflection: It is easy to believe people are fixed: that the harsh stay harsh and the one who sets out to do harm will always be that person. Omar's story refuses that. We all carry something we think is too set to change, a temper, a habit, an old story about ourselves. His life is a quiet invitation to bring that very thing to Allah and let it be turned toward good.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
